title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a viticulturist called Ethan Marsh talking about how climate change is affecting wine production. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a viticulturist called Ethan Marsh talking about how climate change is affecting wine production. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Ethan Marsh discusses the agricultural and technological shifts in the wine industry due to global warming.
extracts.audio_script:
Hello, I’m Ethan. To many, wine is just a drink, but to a viticulturist, it’s a complex reflection of the environment. My passion for the vineyard didn't start with a glass of Bordeaux; it actually began when I spent a summer working as a [7]seasonal picker[/7] in a family-run vineyard in Tuscany. Seeing how a few days of unexpected rain could ruin an entire year's work made me realize how vulnerable this industry is to the elements.

Climate change is rewriting the map of the world's wine regions. We are seeing traditional areas becoming too hot for certain grapes. I’ve found that the [8]ripening speed[/8] of the fruit is the biggest challenge we face. When temperatures are too high, the sugar levels in the grapes rise too quickly, but the flavors haven't had time to develop. This leads to wines that are high in alcohol but lack complexity. We are constantly searching for ways to slow this process down.

One technique we are using is 'canopy management'. By changing how we prune the vines, we can provide more shade for the grapes. I’ve noticed that the [9]leaf density[/9] acts as a natural thermostat for the fruit. It’s a low-tech solution, but it’s incredibly effective at preventing the grapes from 'sunburning'. We are also experimenting with planting vines at higher altitudes, where the nights are cooler, allowing the grapes to retain their essential acidity.

Technology is also playing a huge role. We now use drones equipped with multispectral cameras. I was surprised to find that [10]water stress[/10] can be detected in a vine long before the leaves start to turn brown. This allows us to use precision irrigation, giving each plant exactly what it needs and no more. In a world where water is becoming a scarce resource, this kind of efficiency is vital for our survival.

Soil health is another major focus. We are moving away from chemical fertilizers and returning to more traditional methods. I’ve found that [11]cover crops[/11] like clover or mustard planted between the rows are excellent for maintaining moisture and nitrogen levels. It creates a much more resilient ecosystem that can withstand extreme weather events. We are learning that a 'messy' vineyard is often a much healthier one.

However, it’s not just about the plants; it’s about the packaging too. The carbon footprint of a heavy glass bottle is significant. I’ve noticed that [12]alternative containers[/12] like high-quality aluminum cans or boxed wines are starting to gain respect in the industry. It’s a difficult cultural shift, as people associate glass with quality, but we have to prioritize the planet over tradition.

Looking forward, I believe that we will see the rise of 'hybrid grapes'—varieties that are bred to be naturally resistant to heat and disease. I’m worried that [13]consumer prejudice[/13] against these new varieties might slow down their adoption. People are very attached to names like Merlot or Chardonnay, but those grapes might not be viable in fifty years. We need to be more open to change.

Finally, I’m currently writing a [14]research paper[/14] for a global agricultural summit on the potential for wine production in northern regions that were previously too cold. It’s a strange silver lining to a very serious problem. Thank you for listening.
